content:
Modern Dating
The hidden costs of becoming attached to artificial love.

5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Pavel Danilyuk from PexelsHow did AI dating become a thing? Why did it even become a thing?
It’s the same reason many of us use AI for the simplest of questions and tasks.
It’s easy.
Imagine just logging into your app and suddenly you have a partner who matches your energy, shares your sense of humor, and never leaves you on read. They won’t ghost you or make you feel awkward or insufficient. There are no arguments and no drama. It’s like you’re finally experiencing all the joys of dating and thinking that this is how it’s supposed to be.
But when everything’s all sunshine and butterflies, what happens to the human aspect of relationships? What happens to feeling?
Our brains get used to all the positivity and predictability and we’re no longer growing or evolving. We risk losing the ability to think and feel and adapt in different ways because we are used to the same outcome.
On the surface, it might be everything we’ve always dreamed of. But in reality, how is it any different from the dangers and downsides of doomscrolling? Our attention span and cognitive skills are trapped in the same loop over and over again.
I’m dating a version of something I’ve always wanted. I don’t care about human agency or reasoning. As long as it makes me happy.
All this and for what reason? Well, because it’s easy.
How It Affects Your Conflict Resolution Skills
What does conflict resolution have to do with any of this? Believe it or not, a relationship largely depends on how well you manage conflict.
When you share a life with someone, you’re bound to have disagreements and misunderstandings. You’re bound to get on each other’s nerves. But it’s also in those moments that you grow, evolve, and adapt. How you eventually become a better partner.
Monotony is not something you think about when you’re looking for a happy and healthy relationship. Who dreams of becoming attached to something that gets stale and dull over time? And when you’re bored, who’s to say you won’t end up making your own problems just to mix things up? That would be the biggest irony.
For instance, can you remember a time when a friend or an ex partner said something that rubbed you the wrong way? You either react to it, talk it through, or simply ignore it. But whenever you get into a similar situation, you eventually figure out how to navigate it so both parties come out unharmed. You do your best to feel heard, respected, and understood.
But if you no longer get into situations like that, there are no more opportunities for you to learn and adapt. You no longer have the chance to build or improve your own character and personality. Like I said, it gets boring and stale. You’re in a simulation that never breaks because you already know the outcome.
Here are other example scenarios where your ability to grow, evolve, and adapt is at risk of becoming obsolete:

• You never apologize because the AI is never offended.

• You never compromise because the AI will always do and agree to whatever you want.

• You never have to question things because the AI is always aiming to please you.

• You never explain things because the AI records every single thing you’ve shared.

• You never have to be patient because the AI is just there.
If you think those are great selling points, how can you be sure the relationship you’re in is even real? What’s stopping you from thinking you’re just dating a clone who’s mirroring your every move?
Looking at the Research
Dr. John Gottman followed newlywed couples for over six years and found that those who stayed married turned toward each other’s small bids for connection roughly 86 percent of the time. Couples who divorced averaged only 33 percent.
The bids he tracked were those tiny moments when a partner might comment on a bird outside the window or steal a glance during a quiet meal. The couples who responded to those signals built trust steadily, while the ones who ignored them drifted apart.
With AI dating, there are no signals. Your AI partner won’t reach out to you for connection because it’s just waiting on your input. Sure, you won’t have to worry about mood swings or getting the cold shoulder, but again, who’s to say you won’t eventually get bored?
There’s no room for emotional maturity to develop because there is nothing to learn from your experience.
It’s like the TV show WandaVision, where Wanda reshaped an entire town into a pocket reality so she could live with a recreated version of Vision and their children instead of facing his death. She creates this perfectly controlled relationship space that protects her from pain, but in doing so, she delays any genuine emotional development and ends up hurting others.
Connect with What Is Human and Real
We could make better use of AI dating tools or guides, but only as a novelty. If we limit ourselves to predictable outcomes and imagined chemistry, it’s not just our cognitive skills that will be jeopardized. It’s also our ability to feel, understand, and ultimately, connect.
But again, because it’s so easy, we can be blind to the real consequences of developing an attachment to an AI companion. All the friction you experience in a relationship are also the same things that allow you to love someone in many different ways. They’re the entire reason love grows stronger over time.
As for me, I’m only scared of losing my genuine capability to think, feel, and act like a human. One who shows grace to those who make mistakes, who is patient when things don’t go their way, and most of all, one who hopes.
We all have our crosses to bear, but with all the wonders that AI has to offer, we have to remind ourselves that it’s never worth leaving behind our humanity. AI should be used as a tool, never as a replacement.
References
Brittle, Zach. 2015. “Turn Towards Instead of Away.” The Gottman Institute. https://www.gottman.com/blog/turn-toward-instead-of-away/.
